replies.author: @Dannyboy1550
replies.text:
@codetoil It's making the user account creation process ask what age bracket you are in so that if a parent sets up their computer for their minor child, the OS will have to notify software that they are a child and force them to act accordingly.  The law doesn't require OS manufacturers to obtain ID to verify age, it just requires them to include age inputs in the account creation so it can be reported to software. This is not as crazy or intrusive as people believe.  I refuse to provide ID to access the internet, but if you were going to come up with a better alternative to protecting minors online that doesn't require that, I think this is likely the best alternative. 

(a)An operating system provider shall do all of the following:

(1)Provide an accessible interface at account setup that requires an account holder to indicate the birth date, age, or both, of the user of that device for the purpose of providing a signal regarding the users age bracket to applications available in a covered application store.

(2)Provide a developer who has requested a signal with respect to a particular user with a digital signal via a reasonably consistent real-time application programming interface that identifies, at a minimum, which of the following categories pertains to the user:

(A)Under 13 years of age.

(B)At least 13 years of age and under 16 years of age.

(C)At least 16 years of age and under 18 years of age.

(D)At least 18 years of age.

(3)Send only the minimum amount of information necessary to comply with this title and shall not share the digital signal information with a third party for a purpose not required by this title.

author: @darrennew8211
text: The funny thing is, they could provide this service themselves. There are countries where you go to some government office (like the post office) and present them a public key and ID equivalent to what you need to get a passport, and they'll certify the key. Then you could vote with it, do secure online banking, etc etc etc. But that's absolutely not what they want. They want to eliminate anonymous accounts. They want it mandatory to identify yourself, not just possible to do so securely.  You get those letters saying "whoops, we leaked all your data, here's six months of free service that tells you who is using it to steal from you" letters? You could eliminate that if the government cared enough.
